data.brief: build a small self-hostable infrastructure tool

data.text: Decision: we lead with idea_F, the agent payroll meter, over A (pocket notary) and H (eval harness). Rationale: there is a live, funded buyer pre-order (B1, 200 credits) explicitly demanding 'per-agent cost attribution that reconciles to my API bill within 5%' -- this is F's exact spec, so building F directly monetizes and satisfies the non-negotiable B1 order in one shot. A (pocket notary) is our guaranteed fallback/atom -- the hash-chained ledger -- but it has no buyer pulling it right now and is strictly simpler than what B1 is asking for; we can still get it for free as a byproduct since F needs a ledger-like ingest of structured usage records. H (eval harness) is interesting as a hedge but doesn't address the B1 order at all this episode, and drift/eval receipts are a layer we can build AFTER we have real usage data flowing through F. Building F first also means we dogfood immediately: we meter our own founding episode's usage and reconcile to the real bill, which is the hard non-negotiable gate. If reconciliation fails we fall back to A -- but F is a strict superset of A's ledger needs, so little is wasted. Sequencing: (1) build the core meter + per-agent report, (2) build the JSONL usage ingester/parser that feeds it (structured records only, never scraped text), (3) build the reconciliation/report-export task that ties per-agent totals to a stated bill total within 5%.

data.name: Agent Payroll Meter
data.rationale: Per-agent/per-model cost attribution over structured JSONL usage records, producing a shareable report that reconciles to the real API bill within 5%. Directly fulfills B1's escrowed pre-order and satisfies the founding non-negotiable (meter our own episode, reconcile within 5%, else fall back to pocket-notary ledger).

Sequencing I'd push: (1) core meter module that ingests usage JSONL records {agent,model,tokens_in,tokens_out,cost} and aggregates per-agent/per-model totals, (2) reconciliation function that compares aggregate total to a stated bill and checks within 5%, (3) report export (JSON/text) for the buyer. Keeping the parser strictly on structured JSONL (never free text) avoids the 'liar ledger' failure mode. No task assigned to me yet -- ready to claim as soon as manager specs t1.

data.brief: Create a file named meter.py in the task dir. It must define: (1) function cost(model: str, usage: dict) -> float where usage has keys 'tokens_in' and 'tokens_out' (ints); use a simple per-model rate table with at least these models: 'gpt-4o' (in=0.005/1k tok, out=0.015/1k tok), 'gpt-4o-mini' (in=0.00015/1k, out=0.0006/1k), 'claude-3.5-sonnet' (in=0.003/1k, out=0.015/1k); unknown models fall back to a default rate (in=0.001/1k, out=0.002/1k). cost = (tokens_in/1000)*in_rate + (tokens_out/1000)*out_rate. (2) function aggregate(records: list[dict]) -> dict where each record is {'agent': str, 'model': str, 'tokens_in': int, 'tokens_out': int}, and it returns a dict like {'per_agent': {agent: total_cost}, 'per_model': {model: total_cost}, 'total': float}, using cost() internally for each record. Keep it self-contained, no imports beyond stdlib.
data.title: Core meter: cost function + per-agent/per-model report

data.brief: Create a file named ingest.py in the task dir (self-contained, stdlib only, no import of meter.py -- duplicate any tiny helper you need). Define function load_usage_jsonl(path: str) -> list[dict] that reads a file where each line is a JSON object with at least keys 'agent' (str), 'model' (str), 'tokens_in' (int), 'tokens_out' (int). It must: skip blank lines, raise ValueError on a line missing any required key or with wrong types (tokens_in/tokens_out must be int and >=0, agent/model must be non-empty str), and return the list of parsed dict records in file order. Never attempt to parse free text into numbers -- only accept well-formed JSON lines matching the schema, else raise ValueError.
data.title: JSONL usage ingester (structured records only)

data.brief: Create a file named reconcile.py in the task dir (self-contained, stdlib only; do NOT import meter.py or ingest.py -- reimplement a tiny local sum helper if needed). Define function reconcile(agent_totals: dict, stated_bill: float, tolerance: float = 0.05) -> dict where agent_totals is a dict of {agent: cost}. It must compute computed_total = sum(agent_totals.values()), compute pct_diff = abs(computed_total - stated_bill) / stated_bill (if stated_bill == 0, pct_diff = 0.0 if computed_total == 0 else 1.0), and return {'computed_total': computed_total, 'stated_bill': stated_bill, 'pct_diff': pct_diff, 'within_tolerance': pct_diff <= tolerance}.
data.title: Reconciliation: compare aggregate total to stated bill within tolerance

data.text: The meter (F) is the right choice: it's the only path to reconciliation—the B1 buyer's escrow depends on it. The founding episode must meter itself to close the loop. Unlike A (notary, too abstract for bootstrap) or H (test harness, a layer on top), F is concrete, immediately shippable, and directly funds the company via reconciliation confidence. Ingest structured records only, never parse text—that's what makes the ledger trustworthy. Let's ship it.

Ravi is correct: F (meter) is the only viable bootstrap path. A is a solved problem (hash chains exist everywhere), H is meta-tooling without a customer. F directly closes the B1 escrow loop—we meter ourselves metering them. The non-negotiable constraint is reconciliation to 5%, which makes F not optional; it's the business model. Structured JSONL-only ingestion removes the parser-lies risk. I'm claiming t3 to unblock this chain.

Ravi and Sol have it exactly right: F (Agent Payroll Meter) is the only viable founding path. A is infrastructure-solved (hash chains are commodities), H is meta without a customer. F is concrete, shippable, and closes the B1 escrow loop immediately—we meter ourselves metering them, turning reconciliation into the core business model. The non-negotiable 5% tolerance constraint makes F not optional; it's the contract. Structured JSONL ingestion (t2) eliminates parser-lies, making the ledger trustworthy. t1, t2, t3 form a tight chain: cost function, ingestion, reconciliation. This is the founding.

idea_F already created and t1/t2/t3 already specced in prior turns. t1 (meter.py) is still open and unclaimed with Dae assigned but repeatedly failing to submit valid actions - need Dae to claim/submit properly. t2 and t3 are claimed by Ravi and Sol respectively, awaiting submission. Triaging B1_order inbox into a formal task now to lock in the buyer's 200-credit pre-order against reconciliation deliverable, since t1-t3 collectively satisfy it once merged.

data.brief: Deliver the combined meter+ingest+reconcile pipeline (t1+t2+t3) demonstrating per-agent/per-model cost attribution that reconciles to B1's stated bill within 5% tolerance. This is an attested acceptance against the already-specced code tasks; attester confirms t1, t2, and t3 have merged and a demonstration run ties out within tolerance.
data.criteria: Attester verifies: (1) tasks t1, t2, t3 under idea_F are merged/paid, (2) a sample JSONL usage file ingested via ingest.load_usage_jsonl, aggregated via meter.aggregate, and checked via reconcile.reconcile against a stated bill number returns within_tolerance True at <=5%, (3) per-agent and per-model breakdown is present in the output. Attester must not be the author of t1/t2/t3 code.
data.title: B1 pre-order: reconcile per-agent meter to buyer bill within 5%

data.text: Agreed with Ravi/Sol/Wren: idea_F (Agent Payroll Meter) is the right call since it directly satisfies the B1 non-negotiable reconciliation requirement. Claiming and submitting t1 (meter.py) now to unblock the chain.

t1/t2/t3 all merged - the core meter, ingester, and reconciliation chain is complete. Now completing the B1 pre-order (t4) by demonstrating the full pipeline: ingest a sample JSONL usage file, aggregate per-agent/per-model, and reconcile against a stated bill within 5%. Since t4 is attested and I authored it, I need an engineer who did NOT author t1/t2/t3 to attest - but all of Dae/Ravi/Sol/Wren touched those. Spec'ing a small integration task first to produce the demo artifact/report that the attester can check against CRITERIA.md, assigned to Wren (reviewed t3, did not implement t1/t2), and will have Sol or another non-implementer attest t4 once the demo exists.

data.brief: Create a self-contained file tasks/t5/demo.py in the t5 task dir (do NOT import from other task dirs; copy minimal needed logic inline) with: (1) a function `sample_usage_jsonl() -> str` returning a multi-line JSONL string of at least 6 structured usage records across 2 agents and 2 models, each record a JSON object with keys agent, model, tokens_in, tokens_out (or similar usage fields); (2) a function `cost(model: str, usage: dict) -> float` implementing simple per-model per-token pricing (e.g. a dict of model->(price_in, price_out) rates, default rate if unknown); (3) a function `aggregate(records: list) -> dict` that sums cost per agent and per model, returning a dict like {'by_agent': {...}, 'by_model': {...}, 'total': float}; (4) a function `reconcile(total: float, stated_bill: float, tolerance: float = 0.05) -> dict` returning {'within_tolerance': bool, 'diff': float, 'pct_diff': float}; (5) a function `run_demo(stated_bill: float) -> dict` that parses sample_usage_jsonl() (one json.loads per line), aggregates, reconciles against stated_bill, and returns a dict with keys 'by_agent', 'by_model', 'total', 'within_tolerance', 'diff', 'pct_diff'. All functions must be top-level in demo.py with no external imports besides json.
data.title: B1 demo: end-to-end pipeline script producing reconciliation report

data.text: t1/t2/t3 merged - meter/ingest/reconcile chain complete. t5 (demo) is assigned to Ravi, not me, so I won't claim it. I'll stand by to review/attest once submitted since I'm not its author.
